metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the two-step mechanism in What §2c: use `git cat-file -e` only for the existence test, then use a separate command (e.g. `BASE=$(git cat-file blob refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>)` or `BASE=$(git show refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>)`) to read the actual value, and add the content-read command to the AC grep verification.
evidence: Plan text: 'git cat-file -e refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile> distinguishes: main HAS the file -> read BASE and FAIL with a must override ::error:: if BASE != LIMIT (the lock)'
`git cat-file -e` only sets exit code (0=exists, non-zero=missing); it emits NO content to stdout, so BASE cannot be derived from it alone.
AC grep check verifies `cat-file -e refs/remotes/gh-main` and `must override` as separate substrings, leaving the content-read step unspecified and unverified.
ABSENCE: the plan never names the command to actually read BASE (e.g. `git cat-file blob` or `git show`).
findings.finding: The plan's mechanism description conflates `git cat-file -e` (existence-only exit-code test) with content-reading needed to obtain BASE for the `BASE != LIMIT` comparison. `git cat-file -e` produces no output; reading the limit value from main requires a separate command (`git cat-file blob refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>` or `git show refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>`). An implementer following the plan's wording literally for the existence branch could produce a gate that never actually reads BASE and silently skips the lock check.
findings.location: What §2c: 'git cat-file -e refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile> distinguishes: main HAS the file -> read BASE and FAIL with a must override ::error:: if BASE != LIMIT'
scenarios: An implementer uses only `git cat-file -e` for the existence test and forgets to add a second command to read the file content, causing BASE to be empty/unset and the comparison `BASE != LIMIT` to always be false (lock silently disabled).
An implementer uses `git cat-file -e` expecting it to output content (misreading the flag), producing a gate that errors out on the existence branch.
findings.suggested_fix: Split the description into two explicit sub-steps: (a) `git cat-file -e refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>` to test existence; (b) if exit 0, `BASE=$(git cat-file blob refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>)` to read the value; then compare `[ "$BASE" != "$LIMIT" ]`. Update the AC grep to also check for `cat-file blob` (or `git show`) so the content-read is contractually required.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 2a/2b scope split against the parent: either (a) update parent epic 716f to explicitly authorize a Phase 2a/2b decomposition and add a Phase 2b ticket covering deletion of allowlist/ceilings files, docs/architecture.md pruning, and AGENTS.md update — so those deliverables have an authorized parent-scoped home — or (b) incorporate all of Phase 2's scope into this single leaf so it satisfies parent ACs #2 and #3 as written.
evidence: Parent Decomposition: 'Phase 2 — one child story: delete the allowlist+ceilings mechanism, replace the contract test with an absolute 'nothing over 800' test, prune the architecture.md offender table, and add the limit-lock gate.'
Parent AC #2: 'The allowlist/ceilings mechanism (both files + the sync contract test + the shell gate's allowlist branch) is removed; CI still fails any new over-cap file'
Leaf What item 5: 'KEEP the now-empty allowlist + ceilings files (Phase 2b deletes them) so main's current gate stays green on THIS change's CI'

Leaf Notes: 'Phase 2b (follow-up) deletes the orphaned allowlist/ceilings files, prunes the docs/architecture.md offenders table, and updates AGENTS.md — separate because the gate must stop reading those files (this change) BEFORE they can be deleted.'
Parent file_impact: both '.github/module-size-allowlist.txt' ('drained to empty then removed') and '.github/module-size-ceilings.txt' ('drained then removed') listed as parent deliverables
findings.finding: The leaf explicitly KEEPS the allowlist and ceilings files (What item 5: 'KEEP the now-empty allowlist + ceilings files') and defers their deletion to an unsanctioned 'Phase 2b', directly contradicting parent AC #2 which requires 'The allowlist/ceilings mechanism (both files … ) is removed'. The parent's Decomposition also describes Phase 2 as 'one child story' with no 2a/2b split — meaning Phase 2b has no authorized parent-scoped home.
findings.location: Leaf What item 5 / Notes section ('Phase 2b (follow-up) deletes the orphaned allowlist/ceilings files…'); Parent epic 716f Decomposition ('Phase 2 — one child story') and AC #2 ('both files … is removed')
scenarios: Phase 2a lands: the parent's AC #2 is unmet (both files still present), but the epic may be marked complete, leaving the file deletion permanently deferred.
Phase 2b is never filed: the allowlist/ceilings files, docs/architecture.md offenders table, and AGENTS.md update remain untracked with no parent-authorized story to land them.
Even if Phase 2b is filed later, the parent's 'one child story' framing means it will also fail G7 review against the same parent unless the parent is updated first.
findings.suggested_fix: Update parent epic 716f's Decomposition to read 'Phase 2a — [this story]: rewrite gate + tests, add limit file, keep allowlist/ceilings files temporarily. Phase 2b — [new child story]: delete allowlist/ceilings files, prune docs/architecture.md offenders table, update AGENTS.md.' Also file a Phase 2b ticket. This re-bases the parent's contract to authorize the split and gives Phase 2b an authorized parent-scoped home, resolving the G7 conflict. Then re-review the parent (staling its plan-review attestation) before re-reviewing this leaf.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add AGENTS.md / docs documentation of the admin-lock limit mechanism to this leaf's What and ACs, or update the parent epic to explicitly authorize deferring the 'documented' sub-requirement of AC #3 to an authorized Phase 2b ticket.
evidence: Parent AC #3: 'A hard gate blocks changing the 800 limit through the normal contribution process (admin manual override required); documented'
Leaf Notes: 'Phase 2b (follow-up) … updates AGENTS.md — separate'
Leaf What section: no mention of AGENTS.md or docs/architecture.md updates
Leaf ACs: no criterion verifies that the limit-lock mechanism is documented anywhere
findings.finding: The leaf defers the 'documented' requirement from parent AC #3 ('A hard gate blocks changing the 800 limit through the normal contribution process (admin manual override required); documented') to an unspecified Phase 2b, leaving the documentation of the admin-lock mechanism out of scope for this leaf with no authorized follow-on ticket.
findings.location: Leaf Notes ('Phase 2b … updates AGENTS.md'); Parent AC #3 ('documented')
scenarios: Leaf lands: the admin-lock gate is live in CI but undocumented; contributors have no way to know an admin override is required to change the limit.
Phase 2b never materializes: documentation of the admin-lock requirement is permanently omitted, parent AC #3 remains unmet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact git command used to READ the BASE value from `refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>` after existence is confirmed (e.g., `git show refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>` or `git cat-file blob refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>`) — `git cat-file -e` only checks existence and produces no output.
evidence: Plan: 'main HAS the file -> read BASE and FAIL with a 'must override' ::error:: if BASE != LIMIT (the lock)'
Plan: 'git cat-file -e refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile> distinguishes: main HAS the file -> read BASE'
`git cat-file -e` is an existence check (manpage: 'Exit with zero status if <object> exists') — it prints nothing; a separate read command is required to obtain the BASE value
No `git show` or `git cat-file blob` command is mentioned anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: `git cat-file -e refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>` checks EXISTENCE only (exit code 0/1); it does not output file content. Reading the BASE value for comparison requires a separate `git show refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>` or `git cat-file blob refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>` command, but the plan never specifies how BASE is read — leaving the core comparison mechanism unresolved.
findings.location: What §2c — LIMIT LOCK description
scenarios: An implementer following the plan literally would only have an existence check, not a BASE value to compare against LIMIT — the lock comparison cannot be implemented from the spec as written.

If the implementer guesses `git show refs/remotes/gh-main:<file>` vs `git cat-file blob refs/remotes/gh-main:<file>`, behavior on a missing file differs (show exits 128, cat-file exits 128 too, but error messages differ and the script's error handling may diverge).
findings.suggested_fix: In What §2c, expand: 'Then `git cat-file -e refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>` checks existence; if it exits 0, read the BASE value with `BASE=$(git show refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile> | tr -d "[:space:]")` and FAIL if BASE != LIMIT.'

findings.finding: The leaf splits the parent's Phase 2 ('one child story') into Phase 2a and Phase 2b, deferring three parent-required deliverables — deletion of the allowlist/ceilings files, pruning of docs/architecture.md offenders table, and documentation of the limit-lock (AGENTS.md) — to a follow-on story that does not yet exist in the parent's decomposition. The parent's Decomposition explicitly says 'Phase 2 — one child story' covering all of these items together. Under G7's conflict rule the parent wins: the leaf is delivering a SUBSET that omits parent-required scope without the parent having been updated to authorize the 2a/2b split.
findings.location: Leaf: 'Phase 2a' title + Notes section ('Phase 2b (follow-up)…'); Parent epic 716f: Decomposition section ('Phase 2 — one child story: delete the allowlist+ceilings mechanism, replace the contract test…prune the architecture.md offenders table, and add the limit-lock gate') and ACs ('The allowlist/ceilings mechanism…is removed')
findings.suggested_fix: In epic 716f, revise the Decomposition to read: 'Phase 2a — swap the gate to absolute cap + limit-lock (keeps allowlist/ceilings files temporarily); Phase 2b — delete orphaned allowlist/ceilings files, prune architecture.md offenders table, update AGENTS.md.' Update the 'allowlist/ceilings mechanism is removed' AC to reference Phase 2b completion. Then re-review this leaf against the updated parent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that simulates the bootstrap case: git cat-file returns non-zero (file absent on main) → gate exits 0 (bootstrap allowed), verifying the allow-on-no-base-file branch is exercised and distinct from the fetch-failure warn path.
evidence: Plan: 'main LACKS it -> bootstrap (the file is introduced on THIS change) -> allow'
AC §6 only covers: 'BASE=800 LIMIT=900 -> exit 1 with the override message; BASE=800 LIMIT=800 -> exit 0'
No AC simulates: main ref lacks the limit file → gate exits 0 (bootstrap allowed)

The bootstrap branch is the scenario that fires exactly once in the repo's lifetime and is highest-risk for a silent mis-classification (main has the file but fetch fails could masquerade as bootstrap if the logic is wrong)
findings.finding: The bootstrap branch of the limit-lock logic — 'main LACKS the limit file → allow (first introduction of the file)' — is named in the plan but has no corresponding AC verification step; only the lock-mismatch and lock-match cases are simulated in the ACs.
findings.location: What §2c (bootstrap path) and Acceptance Criteria §6 (simulated lock AC)
scenarios: On the one real bootstrap commit, a logic bug could cause the lock to incorrectly reject or silently skip; without an AC exercising this branch, the gate ships untested for that path
A reviewer or future modifier of the gate script cannot verify the bootstrap path behaves as specified
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC §6: 'Bootstrap: simulate git cat-file -e returning exit 1 (file absent on main ref) → gate exits 0 with a bootstrap-allow message; verify this path does NOT emit the override ::error::.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that §4's update to `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` means REPLACING the existing `"module-size ratchet": "module-size-ceilings.txt"` entry with a new entry (e.g. `"module-size absolute cap": "module-size-limit.txt"`) — not adding alongside it — so the parity test does not break on its own stale signature.
evidence: Plan §4: 'Update `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` to `.github/module-size-limit.txt`'
Existing entry at line 50: `"module-size ratchet": "module-size-ceilings.txt"`
Plan AC: 'reads NEITHER the allowlist NOR the ceilings file' — so `module-size-ceilings.txt` will disappear from both rewritten workflow steps
`test_verified_gate_runs_every_shared_gate` asserts each signature is present in BOTH workflows; a stale `module-size-ceilings.txt` key would therefore fail that test
findings.finding: Plan §4 says to update `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` in `test_ci_workflow_parity.py` to `.github/module-size-limit.txt`, but does not specify that the existing `"module-size ratchet": "module-size-ceilings.txt"` entry must be REMOVED. If left in place, `test_verified_gate_runs_every_shared_gate` will assert that `module-size-ceilings.txt` appears in both workflows — but the plan's own AC explicitly requires the gate reads NEITHER the allowlist NOR the ceilings file, so `module-size-ceilings.txt` will be absent from both rewritten workflows and the parity test breaks on itself.
findings.location: What §4 / tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py line 50
scenarios: Implementer updates `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` by adding a new key for module-size-limit.txt without removing the old ceilings key; the parity test then fails because neither rewritten workflow contains `module-size-ceilings.txt`.
Implementer removes the old key but the plan gave no explicit instruction to do so, leaving ambiguity about intent.
findings.suggested_fix: In §4, write: 'Replace the existing `"module-size ratchet": "module-size-ceilings.txt"` entry with `"module-size absolute cap": "module-size-limit.txt"`' to make the mutation unambiguous.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

verify command `for f in .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml .github/workflows/test.yml; do grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' "$f" || exit 1; ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' "$f" || exit 1; ! grep -q 'module-size-ceilings' "$f" || exit 1; done && echo OK` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote

verify command `for f in ...both...; do grep -q 'github.repository' "$f" && grep -q 'cat-file -e refs/remotes/gh-main' "$f" && grep -q 'could not fetch main' "$f" || exit 1; done && echo OK` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote

verify command `for f in ...both...; do grep -q 'must override' "$f" || exit 1; done && echo OK`)` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote

verify command `for f in ...both...; do grep -q 'must override' "$f" || exit 1; done && echo OK` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace pseudo-code verify clauses in AC 5 and AC 6 with actual runnable shell commands (e.g., a `bash -c '...'` invocation that sources the extracted gate logic against a temp directory, or a named test-helper script that sets up the required env/fs state and asserts the exit code).
evidence: AC 5 verify: 'run the gate step with no limit file -> exit 1 with must not be removed' — no actual shell invocation given
AC 6 verify: 'BASE=800 LIMIT=900 -> exit 1 with the override message; BASE=800 LIMIT=800 -> exit 0' — pseudo-code arrow notation, not a runnable command
E6 verify-command defect taxonomy item (4): fixture invalidity / missing actual executable command
findings.finding: AC 5 ('Gate FAILS when limit file is MISSING') and AC 6 ('A simulated limit change fails the lock') provide pseudo-code behavioral assertions rather than actual runnable shell commands. AC 5 says 'run the gate step with no limit file' without specifying how to invoke the inline shell logic in isolation. AC 6 writes 'BASE=800 LIMIT=900 -> exit 1' — this notation describes expected variable-state behavior but is not an executable command an agent can run.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 5 ('The gate FAILS when the limit file is MISSING') and AC 6 ('A simulated limit change fails the lock')
scenarios: An executing agent cannot confirm the missing-file guard or the lock simulation are working without a real command to run, leading to the AC being rubber-stamped rather than proven.
findings.suggested_fix: For AC 5: `tmpdir=$(mktemp -d) && bash .github/scripts/module-size-gate.sh "$tmpdir" 2>&1; test $? -eq 1` (requires extracting gate logic to a script, which the plan should require). For AC 6 (inline): `BASE=800 LIMIT=900 bash -c 'source .github/scripts/module-size-gate.sh; lock_check'; test $? -eq 1`. Alternatively, add a pytest parametrize test in `test_module_size_contract.py` that mounts a tmp-fs fixture and asserts exit codes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies both `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` and the ceilings file still exist after Phase 2a (e.g. `test -f .github/module-size-allowlist.txt && test -f .github/module-size-ceilings.txt && echo OK`)
evidence: 'KEEP the now-empty allowlist + ceilings files (Phase 2b deletes them) so main's current gate stays green on THIS change's CI'
ABSENCE: no AC of the form 'test -f .github/module-size-allowlist.txt && test -f .github/module-size-ceilings.txt || exit 1'
findings.finding: What item 5 declares 'KEEP the now-empty allowlist + ceilings files' as an explicit deliverable required for CI correctness, but no AC verifies these files still exist after the change is applied.
findings.location: What, item 5 / Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: An implementer deletes the files as part of cleanup, not realising they are needed; the gate passes all stated ACs yet breaks main's current CI run against this change.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add after current AC list:
- [ ] Orphaned files are preserved (verify: `test -f .github/module-size-allowlist.txt && test -f .github/module-size-ceilings.txt && echo OK`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Notes assertion: acknowledge that the explicit-URL git fetch pattern exists only in gerrit-verify.yaml (not in test.yml), and confirm that the LIMIT LOCK fetch will be newly added to test.yml's Module-size gate step (it is technically valid but not a pre-existing pattern in that file).
evidence: gerrit-verify.yaml lines 100-102: git fetch --depth=1 "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" '+tickets:refs/remotes/gh-tickets' — pattern confirmed present
gerrit-verify.yaml lines 489-491: same pattern for verify-identity job
test.yml: grep for 'git fetch' returns NO MATCHES — the explicit-URL fetch pattern does not exist anywhere in test.yml

test.yml line 174: uses: actions/checkout@v7 — standard checkout action that establishes an 'origin' remote, so the stated justification for avoiding 'origin' (checkout-gerrit-change-action does not guarantee one) does not apply to test.yml's CI context
findings.finding: The plan asserts the explicit-URL git fetch pattern for the LIMIT LOCK is 'the identical pattern the tickets-branch fetch (lines ~100/489 of gerrit-verify.yaml) already relies on' and therefore 'proven to work in this CI' — but this pattern exists ONLY in gerrit-verify.yaml, not in test.yml. test.yml uses actions/checkout@v7 (which sets up a normal origin remote) and contains zero git fetch invocations with an explicit HTTPS URL anywhere in the file. The plan's Notes claim is factually accurate for gerrit-verify.yaml's CI context but is an overstatement when extended to test.yml, where the explicit-URL fetch is a new, unestablished pattern.
findings.location: Notes section — 'The explicit-URL fetch is not an unverified assumption — it is the identical pattern the tickets-branch fetch (lines ~100/489 of gerrit-verify.yaml) already relies on, so its availability in this CI is established.'
scenarios: Implementer may wrongly assume test.yml's Module-size gate already has git fetch infrastructure to build on, leading to incomplete or misdesigned lock logic in that workflow.

The LIMIT LOCK's fetch in test.yml is technically sound (git fetch with explicit URL works regardless of remote config), but the plan's 'established precedent' justification is false for that file — a reviewer catching this inconsistency could challenge the plan's correctness more broadly.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Notes, replace 'the identical pattern the tickets-branch fetch (lines ~100/489 of gerrit-verify.yaml) already relies on, so its availability in this CI is established' with 'this pattern is established in gerrit-verify.yaml (lines ~100/489); for test.yml, the git fetch with explicit HTTPS URL is new but technically valid since test.yml uses actions/checkout@v7 which sets up a normal origin remote and git fetch with an explicit URL works independently of any pre-configured remote.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a minimal AGENTS.md update in Phase 2a (alongside items 1–5) that describes the new `.github/module-size-limit.txt` mechanism and admin-lock semantics — the file-deletion ordering constraint does not prevent this partial update, and it closes the doc-gap window between 2a and 2b.
evidence: Plan explicitly defers: 'Phase 2b (follow-up) deletes the orphaned allowlist/ceilings files, prunes the docs/architecture.md offenders table, and updates AGENTS.md'
No AGENTS.md or ADR update is listed in the Phase 2a 'What' items 1–5
The file-deletion ordering constraint cited ('the CI gate runs from MAIN's workflow against each change's tree') applies only to DELETING the allowlist/ceilings files, not to updating AGENTS.md
The admin-lock pattern (main's copy of the file gates changes to it) is a novel architectural decision not documented anywhere in this change
findings.finding: Phase 2a introduces a novel admin-lock pattern and a new config artifact (`.github/module-size-limit.txt`) but defers ALL documentation updates (AGENTS.md, docs/architecture.md) entirely to Phase 2b. The file-deletion ordering constraint that motivates the two-phase split does NOT apply to doc updates: AGENTS.md can be partially updated in 2a to describe the new limit-file mechanism and lock semantics without deleting references to the still-present allowlist/ceilings files. After 2a merges, AGENTS.md will describe the old allowlist-based gate while the gate itself uses a completely different mechanism; a contributor who encounters the 'must override' error has no documented path to understand or comply with the admin-lock contract.
findings.location: Phase 2a plan — 'Notes' section / 'What' section item 5 / 'Phase 2b (follow-up)' paragraph
scenarios: A contributor tries to update the 800 limit via a normal PR, gets 'must override' error, searches AGENTS.md, finds only the old allowlist-based documentation, and cannot determine the correct process
A reviewer auditing the codebase after 2a merges finds AGENTS.md describing a mechanism the gate no longer uses, creating confusion about the authoritative spec
findings.suggested_fix: In 'What', add item 6: 'Update AGENTS.md — add a subsection under the CI-gate description explaining that `.github/module-size-limit.txt` is the single source for the 800-LOC cap, that the gate enforces an admin lock (changes to this file require force-submit past the gate), and that the error message 'must override' means the limit file was modified and must be accompanied by an admin override. Leave existing allowlist/ceilings references in place (Phase 2b removes them).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC that actually executes the rewritten gate shell (e.g., via a Bats/shunit2 test or a `bash` invocation against a crafted `src/rebar/` temp tree) and asserts the correct exit code and error message for each branch (bootstrap, match, mismatch, fetch-failure, missing-file).
evidence: ACs 1–4 are grep/static checks on YAML text; ACs 7–8 are pytest on Python files; none actually executes the shell gate
Notes claim 'oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs' but AC 5 and 6 (the only candidates) give pseudo-code, not tree-based execution

E6(f): 'A cutover/default-flip to a new code path has an acceptance criterion that EXERCISES that path end-to-end as in production' — the new shell gate path is the cutover and CI is its live boundary
The grep ACs (2–4) confirm text is present in YAML but cannot confirm the logic is correct (e.g. an off-by-one in the comparison, a wrong variable name, a missing `||` that silences an error)
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the new shell gate end-to-end as it will run in production CI — i.e., by actually executing the rewritten gate step against a crafted file tree. The ACs rely on: grep (static text presence in YAML), and pytest (Python contract test that reads the limit file and checks src/rebar file sizes). Neither proxy actually runs the gate shell logic through its branches (fetch-main, cat-file, compare BASE vs LIMIT, absolute cap check). The plan's own Notes say 'oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs' but none of the ACs deliver this.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all), Notes ('E5: the gate is shell (oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs)')
scenarios: The gate YAML could have a syntactically present but logically broken lock-check (e.g., comparison direction inverted) and all grep ACs would still pass; only actually running the shell proves correctness.
A wrong branch in the fetch-fallback path could silently allow limit changes through; pytest on Python files would not detect this.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test file `tests/shell/test_module_size_gate.bats` (or a pytest subprocess fixture) that: (1) creates a tmp dir with a fake `src/rebar/foo.py` at N lines, (2) stubs the `git fetch` / `git cat-file` calls, (3) runs the extracted gate script, and (4) asserts exit code and stderr. Reference this as an AC: `bats tests/shell/test_module_size_gate.bats`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan to the plan (e.g., 'revert this commit or temporarily set BASE=LIMIT in the gate to disable the lock') so that a defective Phase 2a can be recovered from without blocking all CI.
evidence: 'Phase 2a is the enforcement SWAP.'
'the gate must stop reading those files (this change) BEFORE they can be deleted'
ABSENCE: no section describes how to revert Phase 2a if the new gate logic has a defect (e.g., the lock fires incorrectly for all contributors).
findings.finding: Phase 2a performs a direct in-place rewrite of the CI gate shell logic (a destructive swap), but the plan provides no explicit rollback or back-out path if the new gate is found to be defective after landing on main.
findings.location: Why / What item 2 / Notes
scenarios: New gate ships with a bug in the limit-lock logic; every subsequent PR fails; team needs to revert but has no documented back-out procedure.
Explicit-URL fetch pattern works in gerrit-verify.yaml but fails in test.yml due to environment differences; lock silently degrades to warn-only and goes unnoticed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection to Notes: 'If Phase 2a is found defective post-merge: (1) git revert <commit> on main — the gate reverts to the allowlist-based check, which is still green because the allowlist/ceilings files are preserved; (2) alternatively, temporarily patch the gate to echo LIMIT=BASE to disable the lock only.' This leverages the already-preserved allowlist/ceilings files as a natural rollback target.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the CI checkout configures the `origin` remote (the GitHub mirror)' for gerrit-verify.yaml, but gerrit-verify.yaml uses `checkout-gerrit-change-action` (a third-party action, not `actions/checkout`) — and no repo file confirms this action sets `origin` to github.com. The LIMIT LOCK's `git fetch -q origin main` step depends on this being true; if `origin` is absent or points elsewhere (e.g., the Gerrit server), the fetch silently fails and the lock permanently fail-opens to a warning.
findings.location: What §2c (LIMIT LOCK) / gerrit-verify.yaml build-and-test job checkout step

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the CI checkout configures the `origin` remote (the GitHub mirror)' to justify using `git fetch -q origin main` in both workflows, but in gerrit-verify.yaml the checkout is performed by `checkout-gerrit-change-action@v1.0.2` (a third-party action that falls back to the Gerrit server URL when the GitHub mirror has replication lag). No existing step in gerrit-verify.yaml uses `git fetch origin <ref>` — steps that need to fetch from GitHub use the explicit URL `https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}` instead. If the action configures `origin` to point to the Gerrit server (or uses a temporary remote), `git fetch -q origin main` in the new gate will fail and fall through to the warn-only path, permanently degrading the limit lock to a no-op in the Gerrit lane.
findings.location: What §2c / gerrit-verify.yaml Module-size gate step
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `git fetch -q origin main` with `git fetch -q "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" main` (the same URL pattern already used in the require-ticket and verify-identity steps of gerrit-verify.yaml). This is self-sufficient regardless of what `origin` is set to and is consistent with the existing pattern.

findings.finding: The LIMIT LOCK mechanism relies on reading `origin/main`'s limit file (to detect if the limit changed vs. main), but neither workflow is verified to have `origin/main` available as a local git ref at the point the gate step runs. In `gerrit-verify.yaml` the checkout action (`checkout-gerrit-change-action`) performs a shallow fetch of the patchset refspec only — `origin/main` may not exist in the local repo. The plan acknowledges the bootstrap case ('origin/main has no limit file yet') but does not address whether `origin/main` will be fetchable once the file lands on main. If the ref is absent, the lock silently skips or errors, defeating the admin-lock guarantee.
findings.location: What / item 2(c): 'LIMIT LOCK — compare against origin/main's limit'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fetch sub-step inside the gate's shell script before the lock comparison: `git fetch origin main --depth=1 2>/dev/null || true` and then read the base limit via `git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt 2>/dev/null`. Explicitly document this fetch in both workflow files' gate step comments.

findings.finding: The limit-lock mechanism (What §2c: 'compare against origin/main's limit') asserts that `origin/main` is a resolvable git ref in the gerrit-verify.yaml runner context, but this is unverified: `checkout-gerrit-change-action` fetches a Gerrit `refs/changes/*` refspec and it is not established whether `origin/main` is available without an explicit `git fetch origin main` step. The plan specifies the lock behavior but not the shell plumbing to make the ref accessible, creating a capability gap that would cause the lock to silently fail or error at runtime in the Gerrit workflow.
findings.location: What section, item 2(c): 'LIMIT LOCK — compare against origin/main's limit'
findings.suggested_fix: Before the Module-size gate step in both workflows, add a fetch step: `git fetch origin main --depth=1 2>/dev/null || true`, then in the lock snippet use `BASE=$(git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt 2>/dev/null || echo '')` and treat an empty BASE as the bootstrap case (skip lock). Document this in the plan's What §2(c).

findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the shell gate's absolute-cap enforcement end-to-end (running the actual shell step against a tree that contains an over-cap file). The Notes section acknowledges 'E5: the gate is shell (oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs)' but the ACs only grep for strings in the workflow files or run Python tests — neither actually executes the shell gate against a crafted tree.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all AC bullets; Notes section ('E5: the gate is shell (oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Simulate cap enforcement: create a temp file of 801 lines; run the gate snippet `LIMIT=800; wc -l <file> | awk ...`; assert exit 1. Create a file of 800 lines; assert exit 0.' This can be a small shell script or an additional pytest parametrized case in the contract test.

findings.finding: The absolute-cap failure path of the new gate script (a file with wc -l > $LIMIT causing an exit 1) has no behavioral acceptance criterion that actually runs the gate against a crafted over-cap tree. The Notes state the oracle is 'running it against crafted trees, per the ACs' but no AC exercises this path; AC2 only greps for the limit-file reference structurally, and AC4 tests the contract on the real codebase (all files already under cap).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 / Notes (E5 sentence)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'The gate rejects an over-cap file: create a temp file with 801 lines under src/rebar/, run the gate step (or the relevant shell snippet), assert exit 1; a 800-line file passes (exit 0). Verify: `python -c "open('/tmp/src/rebar/big.py','w').write('x\n'*801)"` then run the cap-check snippet.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a smoke-test or inline comment in the gate shell script verifying the `git cat-file -e refs/remotes/gh-main:<file>` exit-code contract (0=exists, non-zero=absent), or note the minimum git version required (git ≥ 1.6.3 for `cat-file -e`), so the bootstrap detection is grounded rather than inferred.
evidence: Search for 'cat-file' in .github/ returns zero matches — this subcommand form is not used anywhere in the existing workflows.
Search for 'refs/remotes/gh-main' in .github/ returns zero matches.
The plan asserts: 'git cat-file -e refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>' distinguishes presence/absence — correct per git docs, but unverified in this CI environment.
Evidence class: Partially-verified (standard git plumbing; the mechanism is documented and well-known, but the specific surface is new to this repo's CI).
findings.finding: `git cat-file -e refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile>` is new, unverified CLI usage in this repo's CI — zero matches in the codebase — and the critical bootstrap-vs-lock-violation distinction depends entirely on its exit-code semantics (0 = path exists in tree, non-zero = absent). While this is standard git plumbing, it has never been exercised in these workflows and warrants explicit verification.
findings.location: What / step 2c: 'git cat-file -e refs/remotes/gh-main:<limitfile> distinguishes: main HAS the file -> read BASE and FAIL … main LACKS it -> bootstrap'
scenarios: If the runner's git version predates `cat-file -e <tree-ish>:<path>` support (very old git), the bootstrap detection silently misbehaves — every PR would be treated as a lock violation.
A subtle exit-code difference between 'object not found' and 'ref not found' (fetch failed but ref is present as empty) could collapse the bootstrap path into the lock path.
findings.suggested_fix: In the gate shell, after the fetch succeeds, add a self-check: `git cat-file -t refs/remotes/gh-main 2>/dev/null || true` to confirm the ref resolved, then proceed with `git cat-file -e`. Alternatively, use `git ls-tree refs/remotes/gh-main -- "$limitfile" | grep -q .` as a more readable alternative to `cat-file -e` that is easier to reason about and equally standard.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
